items.q: Did the Montgomery Bus Boycott end segregation across America?
items.correct: No - it desegregated Montgomery's buses after a Supreme Court ruling and energised the movement, but wider segregation survived until the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and beyond.
items.misconception: The Montgomery Bus Boycott ended segregation throughout the United States.
items.random_wrong: The Montgomery Bus Boycott was about equal pay for bus drivers.
items.why: Students treat famous victories as final ones; Montgomery was a local breakthrough that took nearly a decade more campaigning to turn into national law.

items.q: Did Joseph McCarthy prove there were hundreds of communists in the US government?
items.correct: No - McCarthy never substantiated his shifting claims, and his bullying at the televised Army-McCarthy hearings of 1954 led the Senate to censure him.
items.misconception: McCarthy uncovered a vast proven network of Soviet agents inside the government.
items.random_wrong: McCarthy was the director of the FBI during the Red Scare.
items.why: The fear McCarthy generated is confused with evidence; his lists were never verified, which is why 'McCarthyism' now means reckless accusation.

items.q: Did Black Americans only gain the legal right to vote in 1964?
items.correct: No - the 15th Amendment (1870) had granted Black men the vote; the problem was obstacles like literacy tests, which the Voting Rights Act of 1965 targeted.
items.misconception: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 gave Black Americans the vote for the first time.
items.random_wrong: Black Americans were not allowed to vote anywhere until 1970.
items.why: Students conflate the 1964 and 1965 Acts; 1964 attacked segregation and discrimination, while 1965 dismantled the barriers blocking an existing right.

items.q: What did the Supreme Court rule in Brown v Board of Education (1954)?
items.misconception: Buses across America must desegregate immediately
items.random_wrong: Literacy tests for voting were banned
items.why: It's Segregated public schools were unconstitutional. “Buses across America must desegregate immediately” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Whose arrest sparked the Montgomery Bus Boycott in 1955?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Rosa Parks. “Emmett Till” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which senator led the anti-communist accusations of the early 1950s?
items.why: Correct answer: Joseph McCarthy. “John F. Kennedy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did the Civil Rights Act of 1964 do?
items.correct: Banned segregation in public places and discrimination in employment
items.misconception: Gave women the vote for the first time
items.random_wrong: Abolished slavery in the southern states
items.why: Correct answer: Banned segregation in public places and discrimination in employment. “Gave women the vote for the first time” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the March on Washington take place?
items.why: The real figure is 1963 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
